walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Factory Displays
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Azul, where you are a tile artist decorating the walls of the Royal Palace of Evora. Let's get the factories set up first. For four players, place nine round factory displays in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. For three players use seven, and for two players use five.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all one hundred tiles in the box. There are five colors with twenty tiles of each color. Drop every single tile into the cloth bag and give it a thorough shake to mix them up. This bag is your supply for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Factory Displays
walkthrough.text: Now reach into the bag and draw four tiles at random. Place them face-up on one factory display. Repeat this for every factory display on the table until each one has exactly four tiles showing. For a four-player game, that means you will draw thirty-six tiles total to fill all nine factories.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a Board
walkthrough.text: Hand each player a personal player board. Let's look at it together. On the left side you will see five pattern lines, which are rows that hold one, two, three, four, and five tiles respectively. On the right side is the five-by-five wall grid where tiles will be permanently placed. At the bottom is the floor line where penalty tiles go.

walkthrough.title: Place the Scoring Marker
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a scoring marker in their color and places it on the zero space of the score track that runs along the edge of their player board. This will track your points throughout the game as you tile your wall.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Center Area
walkthrough.text: Leave an open space in the middle of the table between the factory displays. This is the center of the table, and it will collect leftover tiles throughout each round. Place the starting player marker in the center area as well. Whoever drafted from the center first last round will go first next round.

walkthrough.title: Determine the First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who most recently visited Portugal goes first. If nobody has, just pick someone. That player takes the starting player marker and places it near their board. Let's begin decorating the palace.

summary.title: Lay Out the Factory Displays
bullets: 4 players → 9 factories, 3 players → 7, 2 players → 5
Place them in the center of the table

bullets: 100 tiles total — 20 each of 5 colors
Put all tiles in the bag and shake to mix

summary.title: Fill the Factory Displays
bullets: Draw 4 random tiles from bag → place face-up on each factory
Repeat until every factory has exactly 4 tiles

summary.title: Give Each Player a Board
bullets: Left side: 5 pattern lines (rows of 1–5 tiles)
Right side: 5×5 wall grid for permanent placement
Bottom: floor line for penalties

summary.title: Place the Scoring Marker
bullets: Each player places scoring marker on 0
Score track runs along the edge of each player board

summary.title: Set Up the Center Area
bullets: Open space in the middle collects leftover tiles
Place the starting player marker in the center

summary.title: Determine the First Player
bullets: Most recent Portugal visitor goes first (or choose randomly)
First player takes the starting player marker

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Every round in Azul follows three phases in order. First is the Factory Offer phase, where players take turns drafting tiles. Second is the Wall-tiling phase, where completed pattern lines score and move tiles onto the wall. Third is the Prepare Next Round phase, where you clean up and refill the factories for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Drafting from a Factory
walkthrough.text: On your turn during the Factory Offer phase, you must draft tiles. Let's start with the most common option. Pick any one factory display and choose a color. Take all tiles of that color from that factory. Every remaining tile on that factory gets pushed into the center of the table. You cannot take just one tile of a color if there are two of the same color on that factory.

walkthrough.title: Drafting from the Center
walkthrough.text: Instead of drafting from a factory, you may take all tiles of one color from the center of the table. Here is the catch: the first player to draft from the center in each round must also take the starting player marker and place it on their floor line. That marker counts as a penalty tile, but it also means you will go first next round.

walkthrough.title: Placing Tiles on Pattern Lines
walkthrough.text: After you draft tiles, you must place them on one of the five pattern lines on the left side of your board. The top line holds one tile, the next holds two, and so on down to five. All tiles you draft in a single turn must go on the same pattern line. If you take three blue tiles, all three go on one row.

walkthrough.title: The Color Restriction
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most important rule for pattern lines. You cannot place a color on a pattern line if that color already appears in the corresponding row on your wall. Look at your wall grid on the right side of your board. Each row has five predetermined color positions. If blue is already on the wall in row three, you can never place blue tiles on pattern line three again. This is the rule new players forget most often.

walkthrough.title: Overflow and the Floor Line
walkthrough.text: If you draft more tiles than your chosen pattern line can hold, the overflow tiles go straight to your floor line at the bottom of your board. The floor line is bad news. Each space carries an increasing penalty: the first two spaces cost one point each, the next three cost two points each, and the last two cost three points each. Any tiles that overflow past the seventh floor space are discarded with no additional penalty.

walkthrough.title: End of the Factory Offer Phase
walkthrough.text: Players keep taking turns drafting tiles until every factory display is empty and no tiles remain in the center. Every player will have taken multiple turns this phase. Once all tiles are claimed, the Factory Offer phase ends and everyone moves to Wall-tiling at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Wall-Tiling Phase
walkthrough.text: Now check each of your five pattern lines from top to bottom. If a pattern line is completely full, take the rightmost tile from that line and place it on the matching space in the corresponding row of your wall. The remaining tiles from that completed line are discarded into the box lid. Pattern lines that are not completely full stay as they are for next round.

walkthrough.title: Scoring a Placed Tile
walkthrough.text: Every tile you place on the wall scores immediately. If the tile is completely isolated with no neighbors horizontally or vertically, it scores one point. If it is adjacent to other tiles, count the connected group horizontally and the connected group vertically. You score one point for each tile in each connected group, including the tile you just placed.

walkthrough.title: Floor Line Penalties
walkthrough.text: After scoring your wall tiles, apply floor line penalties. The seven floor spaces cost negative one, negative one, negative two, negative two, negative two, negative three, and negative three points. Subtract the total from your score. Your score can never drop below zero. Then clear your floor line and discard those tiles.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Next Round
walkthrough.text: Clear all completed pattern lines by discarding the leftover tiles to the box lid. Leave any incomplete pattern lines untouched for next round. Refill all factory displays with four tiles each from the bag. If the bag runs empty, refill it with all the tiles from the box lid and continue drawing. Place the starting player marker back in the center.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends at the end of the round in which any player completes at least one horizontal line of five tiles on their wall. That means filling an entire row from left to right. You still finish the current round, including Wall-tiling and scoring, before the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Final Bonus Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the final round, add bonus points. Each complete horizontal line on your wall scores two extra points. Each complete vertical column scores seven extra points. Each complete color set, meaning all five tiles of one color placed on your wall, scores ten extra points. These bonuses can dramatically change the final standings.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The player with the highest score wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most complete horizontal lines wins. If it is still tied, the players share the victory. Now let's go practice with a tutorial round.

summary.title: Round Structure
bullets: Phase 1: Factory Offer — draft tiles from factories or center
Phase 2: Wall-tiling — completed pattern lines → score → wall
Phase 3: Prepare Next Round — clean up, refill factories

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through the first round of a four-player game together. All nine factory displays are filled with four tiles each, and the starting player marker sits in the empty center. We will follow two players closely and watch them make real decisions, including some mistakes that will teach us important lessons.

walkthrough.title: Player One Drafts Smart
walkthrough.text: Player one looks at the factories and spots one with three blue tiles and one red tile. Let's take all three blue tiles and place them on pattern line three, which holds exactly three tiles. A perfect fit with no overflow. The one red tile left behind gets pushed to the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Gets Greedy
walkthrough.text: Player two sees a factory with two yellow tiles and two white tiles. Let's say they want yellow, so they take both yellow tiles. But then they spot the center pile growing and decide to also draft from a different factory on this same turn. Wait, you can only draft once per turn. Let's back up and place those two yellow tiles on pattern line two. Perfect fit.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Causes Overflow
walkthrough.text: Player three finds a factory with four red tiles. All four of the same color, what a haul. Let's place them on pattern line two, which only holds two tiles. Two tiles fill the line completely, but the other two overflow directly to the floor line. That is negative two points already. Player three could have placed them on pattern line four or five instead and avoided the penalty entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Count Before You Commit
walkthrough.text: Before you draft tiles, always count how many you are about to take and check whether your chosen pattern line has enough empty spaces. Overflow tiles go straight to the floor line and cost you points. If the number does not fit, pick a larger pattern line or draft a different color with fewer tiles on that factory.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Takes from the Center
walkthrough.text: Several turns have passed and tiles have been piling up in the center. Player four decides to grab three blue tiles from the center. But since player four is the first person to draft from the center this round, they must also take the starting player marker and place it on their floor line. That is one extra penalty slot filled, but player four will go first next round.

walkthrough.title: Drafting Continues Until Empty
walkthrough.text: Let's fast-forward through the rest of the Factory Offer phase. Players keep taking turns, one draft per turn, until every factory is empty and no tiles remain in the center. Everyone now has tiles filling various pattern lines, and some unlucky players have tiles on their floor lines too.

walkthrough.title: Wall-Tiling: Player One Scores
walkthrough.text: Now we move to the Wall-tiling phase. Player one filled pattern line three with three blue tiles earlier. Let's take the rightmost blue tile and place it on the matching blue space in row three of the wall. The other two blue tiles from that pattern line are discarded. If the placed tile has no neighbors, it scores one point. Player one moves their scoring marker up one.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Makes an Illegal Move
walkthrough.text: Player two completed pattern line one with a yellow tile and moved it to the wall in round one. Now in round two, player two tries to place yellow tiles on pattern line one again. Stop right there. Yellow is already on the wall in row one. You can never place a color on a pattern line if that color already occupies the matching wall row. Player two must choose a different line for those yellow tiles or draft a different color entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check the Wall First
walkthrough.text: Before placing drafted tiles on any pattern line, look at the corresponding row on your wall. If that color is already there, that pattern line is permanently closed to that color. This is the most common illegal move new players attempt. Get in the habit of scanning your wall before every placement.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Ignores Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Player three has been placing tiles on the wall in scattered positions, one tile here, another tile way over there. Each isolated tile only scores one point. Meanwhile player one has been placing tiles next to each other. Let's look at player one's wall now. When player one places a tile that connects to two tiles horizontally and one tile vertically, that single placement scores three points for the horizontal group plus two points for the vertical group, totaling five points.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Adjacency Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Tiles placed next to each other on the wall score exponentially more than isolated tiles. A single tile alone scores one point, but a tile placed in a cluster can score five, six, or even eight points at once. Always think about where your tile will land on the wall and which neighbors it will connect to before you choose a pattern line.

walkthrough.title: Floor Line Pain
walkthrough.text: Let's count up player three's floor line penalties. They have the starting player marker plus three overflow tiles, filling four spaces. The penalties are negative one, negative one, negative two, and negative two, for a total of negative six points. That wipes out almost all the points player three scored this round. Player one, who kept a clean floor line, pulls ahead significantly.

walkthrough.title: Preparing Round Two
walkthrough.text: Let's clean up for the next round. Discard the leftover tiles from all completed pattern lines into the box lid. Leave any incomplete pattern lines as they are. Refill all nine factory displays with four tiles each from the bag. The starting player marker goes back to the center. Player four, who picked it up last round, will draft first this round.

walkthrough.title: Looking Ahead to Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and think about the endgame. Completing a horizontal row of five tiles on the wall triggers the end of the game and earns two bonus points. A full vertical column earns seven bonus points, and collecting all five tiles of one color anywhere on the wall earns ten bonus points. These bonuses are massive. Start planning for them now, not in the final round.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text:
Congratulations, you have walked through the complete flow of an Azul round. You saw how overflow punishes greedy drafting, how the wall color restriction blocks careless placement, and how adjacency scoring rewards thoughtful tile positioning. You are ready to play a full game.

First, check out General Tips for drafting advice and scoring strategies.

Second, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime by tapping the Q&A tab during your game.

walkthrough.title: Draft with Your Wall in Mind
walkthrough.text: Before you grab tiles from a factory, look at your wall. Find the row where that color belongs, then check what is already adjacent to that space. The best drafts are the ones that place tiles next to existing clusters on your wall, scoring three, four, or five points instead of just one. Never draft without knowing exactly where the tile will land on your wall.

walkthrough.title: Avoid Floor Line Penalties
walkthrough.text: The floor line is the biggest point drain in Azul. Taking too many tiles and overflowing costs you up to fourteen points per round if you fill the entire floor line. Always count the tiles you are about to take and compare that number to the empty spaces on your target pattern line. If the math does not work, pick a different factory or a different color.

walkthrough.title: Use the Center Strategically
walkthrough.text: New players avoid the center of the table because of the starting player marker penalty. But the center often accumulates large groups of a single color, making it the best drafting spot in the late phase. Let other players push tiles to the center early, then grab a big haul when the timing is right. One or two penalty points from the marker is worth it if you score six or eight points from the tiles you took.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Need
walkthrough.text: Azul is not solitaire. Pay attention to which colors your opponents are collecting and which pattern lines they are filling. If you see an opponent one tile away from completing a high-value pattern line, consider hate-drafting that color to deny them the completion. Pushing unwanted tiles to the center can also disrupt their plans.

walkthrough.title: Think About Bonuses Early
walkthrough.text: The final bonuses are worth two, seven, and ten points respectively for rows, columns, and color sets. A single completed column is worth seven points, which is more than most players score in an entire round of wall-tiling. Start building toward at least one column and one color set from the very first round. The players who plan for bonuses early consistently win.

walkthrough.title: Match Pattern Line Size to Available Tiles
walkthrough.text: The ideal draft fills a pattern line in a single turn with zero overflow. A pattern line of three wants exactly three tiles, and a pattern line of five wants five. When scanning the factories, look for color counts that match the empty spaces on your pattern lines. This keeps your floor line clean and your scoring efficient.

walkthrough.title: Keep Incomplete Pattern Lines Working for You
walkthrough.text: Incomplete pattern lines carry over between rounds, which means a pattern line with two tiles on it only needs one more next round. This is powerful. Partially fill larger pattern lines early so you can complete them cheaply in later rounds. A five-space line filled to four tiles is a guaranteed easy completion next round with just a single tile of that color.

summary.title: Draft with Your Wall in Mind
bullets: Check where the color lands on your wall before drafting
Prioritize tiles that land adjacent to existing clusters
Adjacent placement = 3-5 pts vs. isolated = 1 pt

summary.title: Avoid Floor Line Penalties
bullets: Max floor penalty = -14 pts per round
Count tiles vs. empty pattern line spaces before drafting
If it doesn't fit → pick a different factory or color

summary.title: Use the Center Strategically
bullets: Center accumulates large single-color groups over time
Starting marker penalty (-1 pt) is often worth the big haul
Let others push tiles to center early, then grab late

summary.title: Watch What Opponents Need
bullets: Track opponents' pattern lines and wall progress
Hate-draft colors opponents need to deny completions
Push unwanted colors to center to disrupt plans

summary.title: Think About Bonuses Early
bullets: +2 row, +7 column, +10 color set — plan from round 1
1 complete column = 7 pts (more than most full rounds of scoring)
Players who plan bonuses early win consistently

summary.title: Match Pattern Line Size to Available Tiles
bullets: Ideal: draft fills a pattern line exactly with 0 overflow
Scan factories for color counts matching empty spaces
Clean floor line = efficient scoring

summary.title: Keep Incomplete Pattern Lines Working for You
bullets: Incomplete lines carry over between rounds
Partially fill large lines early → cheap completion next round
A 5-space line at 4 tiles = only 1 tile needed to complete

walkthrough.title: Column-First Strategy
walkthrough.text: Completing vertical columns is the most efficient path to high scores because each column awards seven bonus points. Focus your first several rounds on filling tiles that share a column on the wall. Since each row has each color in a fixed position, you can map out exactly which five colors you need for a column and prioritize drafting those colors to the corresponding pattern lines.

walkthrough.title: Color Set Racing
walkthrough.text: Completing all five tiles of a single color on the wall earns ten bonus points, the largest single bonus in the game. To achieve this, you need to place that color in all five rows, which means filling pattern lines one through five with that color across multiple rounds. Start with your target color on the small pattern lines early, because those complete fastest and build early adjacency clusters on the wall.

walkthrough.title: Denial Drafting
walkthrough.text: In a competitive game, sometimes the best draft is the one that hurts your opponents most. If an opponent needs two red tiles to complete pattern line four, and you can take those reds even at the cost of some floor line penalty, the denial may be worth more than whatever tiles you would have taken for yourself. Advanced players constantly weigh their own scoring against the damage of letting opponents complete high-value lines.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Center Tempo
walkthrough.text: The center of the table is a shared resource that grows throughout the phase. Advanced players manipulate what goes to the center by choosing which factories to draft from and which leftovers to push. If you can flood the center with a color your opponents do not want, they face a dilemma: take the starter penalty for bad tiles or leave them. Meanwhile, you draft what you need from factories without touching the center at all.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Row Completion
walkthrough.text: Completing a row triggers the end of the game, so timing matters. If you are ahead, try to finish a row quickly to end the game before opponents catch up. If you are behind, avoid completing rows and keep the game going longer. Watch opponents' walls carefully. If someone is one tile away from a row, you may want to deny that color or rush your own row to control when the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Adjacency Cluster Planning
walkthrough.text: The most points-per-tile come from placing tiles into large clusters on the wall. A tile placed adjacent to a group of three horizontally and two vertically scores four plus three, or seven points from a single tile. Plan your cluster growth deliberately. Map out which pattern lines feed which wall positions, and choose your drafts to build one large interconnected group rather than scattered individual tiles.

walkthrough.title: First-Player Marker as a Tool
walkthrough.text: Going first next round is genuinely powerful because you get first pick of the freshly filled factories. Advanced players sometimes deliberately take the starting player marker early in a round when the center has only one or two tiles, accepting a small penalty to guarantee first choice next round. The first pick in a round with nine full factories is often worth more than the one-point floor penalty.

summary.title: Column-First Strategy
bullets: Each complete column = +7 bonus pts
Map out which 5 colors fill a column → draft those systematically
Columns also build adjacency clusters naturally

summary.title: Color Set Racing
bullets: All 5 of 1 color on the wall = +10 bonus pts
Requires placing that color in all 5 rows (pattern lines 1–5)
Start with small pattern lines → fastest to complete

summary.title: Denial Drafting
bullets: Take tiles opponents need, even at a floor line cost
Weigh your scoring vs. opponent's blocked points
Especially powerful near end of a round

summary.title: Controlling the Center Tempo
bullets: Choose which factories to draft → controls what goes to center
Flood center with colors opponents don't want
Forces opponents into bad drafts or starter marker penalty

summary.title: End-Game Row Completion
bullets: Completing a row triggers game end → use timing as a weapon
Ahead? Rush a row. Behind? Avoid completing rows
Deny opponents their final row tile to extend the game

summary.title: Adjacency Cluster Planning
bullets: Tile in a 3-wide + 2-tall cluster = 7 pts from 1 placement
Build 1 large interconnected group, not scattered tiles
Map pattern lines → wall positions → plan cluster growth

summary.title: First-Player Marker as a Tool
bullets: Going first = first pick of freshly filled factories
Take marker early when center has few tiles → small penalty
First pick of 9 full factories often worth the -1 pt cost

entries.definition: One of 100 square resin tiles. There are 5 colors with 20 tiles each. Tiles are drawn from the cloth bag to fill factory displays each round. After scoring, leftover tiles go to the box lid and eventually get recycled back into the bag.

A round cardboard disc placed in the center of the table. Each factory holds exactly 4 tiles drawn from the bag at the start of a round. The number of factories depends on player count: 5 for 2 players, 7 for 3 players, 9 for 4 players.

Each player's personal board with three areas: the pattern lines on the left (5 rows holding 1–5 tiles), the wall grid on the right (a 5×5 grid with predetermined color positions), and the floor line at the bottom (7 penalty spaces).

A small cube placed on the score track running along the edge of each player board. Starts at 0 and advances as tiles are placed on the wall. Moves backward for floor line penalties but can never go below 0.

A large tile placed in the center of the table at the start of each round. The first player to draft from the center must take this marker and place it on their floor line as a penalty. That player goes first in the next round.

A cloth drawstring bag holding all 100 tiles at the start of the game. Tiles are drawn randomly to fill factory displays each round. When the bag is empty, discarded tiles from the box lid are returned to refill it.

Take all tiles of one chosen color from a single factory display. The remaining tiles on that factory are pushed to the center of the table. You must take all tiles of the chosen color — no partial drafts allowed.

Take all tiles of one chosen color from the center of the table. The first player to draft from the center each round must also take the starting player marker and place it on their floor line as a penalty.

The shared area in the middle where leftover tiles from factories accumulate during the Factory Offer phase. Also holds the starting player marker at the beginning of each round. Grows larger as players draft from factories and push remainders here.

When a tile is placed on the wall, it scores based on connected neighbors. Count the contiguous horizontal group of tiles (including the placed tile) and the contiguous vertical group. Score 1 point per tile in each group. An isolated tile with no neighbors scores 1 point total.

The 7 spaces at the bottom of the player board. Overflow tiles and the starting player marker go here. Penalties increase across the spaces: -1, -1, -2, -2, -2, -3, -3. Maximum penalty per round is -14 points. Score cannot drop below 0.

Scored after the final round. Complete horizontal line of 5 on the wall = +2 points. Complete vertical column of 5 = +7 points. All 5 tiles of one color placed on the wall = +10 points. Multiple bonuses can stack.

One of 5 rows on the left side of the player board, holding 1 to 5 tiles. Only one color per line. When a line is completely filled, the rightmost tile moves to the wall during the Wall-tiling phase. Incomplete lines carry over between rounds.

When all spaces in a pattern line are filled with tiles of the same color, it is complete. During the Wall-tiling phase, the rightmost tile moves to the matching wall position and the rest are discarded. Only complete lines score.

When drafted tiles exceed the remaining capacity of a chosen pattern line, excess tiles go to the floor line. For example, taking 4 tiles and placing them on a pattern line with only 2 empty spaces means 2 tiles overflow to the floor line.

The 5×5 grid on the right side of the player board. Each space has a predetermined color. Tiles move here from completed pattern lines. Once placed, a tile is permanent. Scoring is based on adjacency to neighboring tiles.

A color that already appears on the wall in a given row cannot be placed on the corresponding pattern line again. Since each wall row has fixed color positions, each color can only appear once per row. This progressively limits options as the game progresses.

A complete row of 5 tiles across one row of the wall. Completing any horizontal line triggers the end of the game at the end of that round. Each complete horizontal line also earns a +2 point bonus. Most complete horizontal lines is the tiebreaker.

A complete column of 5 tiles running top to bottom on the wall. Does not trigger game end but earns a +7 point bonus. Columns are harder to complete than rows because they require placing 5 different colors across 5 different pattern lines.

The first phase of each round. Players take turns drafting tiles from factory displays or the center of the table and placing them on their pattern lines. Continues until all factories and the center are empty.

The second phase of each round. All players simultaneously check their pattern lines. Complete lines move their rightmost tile to the wall and score by adjacency. Floor line penalties are then applied. Incomplete lines remain.

The third phase of each round. Discard leftover tiles from completed pattern lines to the box lid. Refill all factory displays with 4 tiles each from the bag. Return the starting player marker to the center. If the bag is empty, refill it from the box lid.

All 5 tiles of a single color placed on the wall, one in each row. Earns a +10 point end-game bonus. Since each row has fixed color positions, completing a color set means filling a specific diagonal pattern across all 5 rows.
